era_context: Fourth Dynasty Egypt under Khufu; Great Pyramid of Giza in construction. Mesopotamia in Early Dynastic Period III. Indus Valley in Early Harappan phase. This year falls within the era of Old Kingdom Egypt's greatest monumental achievement.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia beyond the Indus Valley

events.title: Great Pyramid of Giza construction continues under Khufu
events.description: The Great Pyramid remains under construction during Khufu's reign. The project continues to mobilise vast labour forces and resources. The pyramid's construction involves the quarrying, transport, and placement of approximately 2.3 million limestone blocks. By 2563 BCE, the pyramid is in its mid-to-late construction phases. The internal structure, including the King's Chamber and Grand Gallery, is being completed or has been completed.
events.certainty_note: The pyramid's existence and Khufu's attribution are archaeologically confirmed. The precise year of construction is uncertain (±5–10 years).

events.title: Egyptian Fourth Dynasty: consolidation of state power and religious authority
events.description: During Khufu's reign, the Egyptian state reaches a peak of centralised power and religious authority. The pharaoh is venerated as a living god, and the state controls vast resources, labour, and religious institutions. The construction of the Great Pyramid is a manifestation of this power. The state's administrative apparatus, including scribes, officials, and overseers, manages the economy, agriculture, and labour mobilisation. This period represents the height of Old Kingdom stability and centralisation.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Giza and Memphis

events.certainty_note: The existence of centralised state power is confirmed by archaeological and epigraphic evidence. Specific details for 2563 BCE are not documented.
cross_references: Part of broader Old Kingdom political system

events.title: Mesopotamian Early Dynastic Period III: city-state competition and trade networks
events.description: In southern Mesopotamia, city-states compete for control of irrigation resources and trade networks. Lagash, Uruk, and Ur are major centres. Trade extends to the Persian Gulf, the Indus Valley, and beyond. Cuneiform writing is used for administrative and commercial purposes. The period is characterised by political fragmentation and frequent conflicts.
key_figures: Rulers of Lagash, Uruk, and Ur (names and dates uncertain)
events.certainty_note: The ED III period is confirmed archaeologically. Specific events for 2563 BCE are not documented. Mesopotamian chronology remains uncertain.
cross_references: Part of Early Dynastic Period III (c. 2600–2334 BCE)

events.title: Indus Valley Early Harappan phase: continued settlement development
events.description: The Early Harappan phase continues, with settlements showing increasing standardisation and planning. Trade connections with Mesopotamia and the Persian Gulf are evident. The proto-urban centres are developing the social and economic structures that will characterise the Mature Harappan phase.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Indus Valley sites

events.certainty_note: The Early Harappan phase is archaeologically confirmed. Specific events for 2563 BCE are not documented.
cross_references: Part of Early Harappan phase (c. 3300–2600 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The chronological uncertainties noted for previous years apply equally to 2563 BCE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 2563 BCE is similar to that for 2560–2562 BCE. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward Egypt and monumental contexts. The lives of ordinary people and non-elite settlements are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: Indus Valley Early Harappan development continues
